task: I had a sketchy call about an account issue, and I’m trying to decide what I should change right away versus what’s probably overkill. Can you help me make a short checklist so I don’t make things worse by reacting too fast?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a **short, practical checklist** for after a sketchy account-related call, focused on **what to do right away** vs **what may be overkill**.

## Do right away
- **Don’t use any info from the call** to log in or “verify” anything.
- **Hang up / stop responding** if the caller pressures you or asks for codes, passwords, or remote access.
- **Change your password** for the affected account **if you suspect they may know it**.
- **Turn on 2-factor authentication (2FA)** if it isn’t already enabled.
- **Check recent account activity**: logins, password changes, email/phone changes, linked devices.
- **Contact the company using the official number or app** to confirm whether the call was real.
- **Watch for follow-up phishing** by text or email pretending to be support.

## Do soon if the account is important
- **Update recovery email and phone number** if they might have been exposed.
- **Review connected apps / devices** and remove anything you don’t recognize.
- **Change passwords on other accounts** if you reused the same or similar password.
- **Freeze your credit** if the account involved banking, loans, or identity info.
- **Alert your bank/card issuer** if money or payment details may be involved.

## Usually overkill unless there’s strong evidence of compromise
- Changing **every** password you own.
- Factory-resetting your phone or computer.
- Cancelling cards or closing accounts immediately.
- Publicly posting about it or replying to the caller again.

## Quick rule of thumb
If the caller asked for:
- **passwords**
- **one-time codes**
- **remote access**
- **payment**
- **personal ID info**

…treat it as suspicious and secure the account first.

If you want, I can turn this into a **1-minute emergency checklist** or tailor it to **banking, email, or social media**.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty much, but I want it tighter. The “turn on 2FA” and “change password” part is fine, but only if it’s the real site or app — not from anything in the call. And I wouldn’t call freezing credit “soon” unless it was a bank or identity thing, not just some random account.

Make it shorter. I’d want:
- stop talking to the caller
- use the official company number only
- change password only if that account is actually the one at risk
- check recent logins / contact info
- watch for follow-up texts or emails

And put the overkill stuff separate, because that’s the part people always overdo when they’re rattled.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter version:

## Do right away
- **Stop talking to the caller** if anything feels off.
- **Use only the official company number/app/site** to verify the issue.
- **Change the password** only for the account that’s actually at risk, and only through the real site/app.
- **Check recent logins and contact info** for changes you don’t recognize.
- **Watch for follow-up texts/emails** that try to keep the scam going.

## Usually overkill unless there’s clear risk
- Freezing credit
- Changing every password you have
- Resetting devices
- Closing accounts or canceling cards right away

If you want, I can make this even shorter into a **5-line “panic-proof” version**.

user_simulator.text: Yeah. That’s the right shape. Short, and it keeps the panic stuff out of it.
